nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and sandy substrates in moderate-current river stretches and tributaries of the Congo River basin; associated with crevices, caves, and submerged root tangles

decorPreferences: caves and rockwork for daytime shelter
dim areas with low direct lighting
PVC pipes or clay pots accepted as cave substitutes

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods; sinking pellets, wafers (algae and catfish wafers), frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and chopped earthworms. Scavenges leftover food from the substrate at night. Vegetable matter such as blanched zucchini and cucumber is relished. Feed after lights out to ensure adequate intake given the strongly nocturnal feeding habit.

goodWith: medium to large Congo tetras (Phenacogrammus interruptus)
other robust Congo basin community fish
larger Synodontis species of similar size
avoidWith: small tetras and nano fish (predation risk at night)
other Synodontis that are significantly smaller (bullying)
fish requiring copper-based medication in shared systems
slow long-finned fish vulnerable to fin harassment

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Synodontis flavitaeniatus showing lateral body stripes. Confirm license on page before use.
Wikimedia Commons image of S. flavitaeniatus in aquarium. Verify attribution and dimensions before use.

summary: The Orangestriped Squeaker is a robust, nocturnal Congo basin catfish distinguished by vivid longitudinal yellow-orange stripes on a dark body. Reaching ~15 cm, it is an intermediate-level species that requires caves, dim lighting, and nighttime feeding; named for the audible stridulation it produces when handled.
careNotes:
Synodontis flavitaeniatus is one of the more visually striking members of the squeaker catfish family Mochokidae, named for its bold yellow-orange horizontal stripes running along a dark olive-to-brown body. Like most Synodontis, it is strictly nocturnal — it will hide throughout the day in caves, under driftwood, or in dense cover, and becomes active only after the tank lights dim. Feeding must be timed accordingly; sinking foods placed after lights-out will be consumed readily, whereas daytime offerings may be ignored or claimed by faster tankmates before the fish emerges.

A minimum tank of 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is advised to provide adequate swimming space and room for territory. Provide multiple caves and overhangs — a single specimen will claim a preferred retreat and defend it. Fine or smooth substrate (sand or fine gravel) protects the delicate barbels. Congo basin conditions suit this species well: slightly soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 4–15) and a temperature of 24–28°C. Good filtration with moderate flow is beneficial; avoid stagnant conditions.

This species is copper-sensitive — catfish of the family Mochokidae lack true scales and can be harmed by copper-based treatments used against parasites. If disease treatment is required, use copper-free alternatives and always remove the fish or use a dedicated hospital tank. Standard aquarium medications should be checked for copper content before use.

When handled or netted, S. flavitaeniatus produces an audible squeaking or grunting sound via stridulation of the pectoral-fin spines — this is harmless but can startle the handler. The pectoral spines themselves are sharp and can cause minor lacerations; use a net with care and avoid bare-hand contact with a distressed fish. Conspecific aggression is low to moderate; a single specimen is typically the easiest option, though groups can be kept in larger tanks (300 L+) with ample cave territory per individual.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Synodontis flavitaeniatus is rarely documented and considered difficult. In the wild, spawning is believed to be triggered by seasonal water-level fluctuations and temperature drops associated with the Congo rainy season. No reliable aquarium-breeding protocol is established for this species; most hobbyist specimens are wild-caught or F1 from specialist breeders. Hormone-induced spawning has been attempted experimentally but is not a practical hobbyist technique. Sexing is difficult externally; females may appear slightly broader across the body when gravid. Egg and fry details are not well documented for home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis flavitaeniatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Congo basin), nocturnal behaviour, diet, and husbandry notes including cave requirements and copper sensitivity warning for Mochokidae.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis flavitaeniatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 15 cm TL), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), native range (Congo River system, DRC and Republic of Congo), temperature range, and diet classification. Confirmed omnivore status and Congo basin distribution.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care
additional.notes: General Synodontis care guide covering nocturnal behaviour, cave/shelter requirements, feeding strategy (lights-out feeding), copper sensitivity of scaleless catfish, and compatibility with community fish. Tank size and diet recommendations corroborate SeriouslyFish data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis species guide
additional.notes: Species-level coverage of S. flavitaeniatus within broader Synodontis guide. Notes on the distinctive longitudinal stripe colouration, stridulation behaviour when handled, pectoral-spine caution, and Congo basin biotope. Confirms intermediate care level and cave-territory requirements.

additional.name: Seriously Fish / Planet Catfish — Synodontis flavitaeniatus species profile
additional.notes: PlanetCatfish profile supplementing breeding difficulty information and wild collection notes. Confirms rare captive breeding, soft slightly-acidic water preference, and that most trade specimens are wild-caught from the Congo basin.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding extremely rare; no reliable aquarium protocol established. Seasonal spawning in the wild tied to Congo rainy-season water-level changes. Hormone induction has been attempted but is not practical for hobbyists. Most trade fish are wild-caught.
